tagline: Calculate pH from hydrogen ion concentration
keywords: ph calculator, hydrogen ion concentration, acid base calculator, ph scale
calcFn: if (hConc <= 0) return; var ph = -Math.log10(hConc); var poh = 14 - ph; var ohConc2 = Math.pow(10, -poh); var cls = ph < 6.5 ? 'Acidic' : ph > 7.5 ? 'Basic (Alkaline)' : 'Neutral'; document.getElementById('ph').textContent = fmt(ph, 2); document.getElementById('poh').textContent = fmt(poh, 2); document.getElementById('ohConc').textContent = ohConc2.toExponential(2) + ' mol/L'; document.getElementById('acidBase').textContent = cls;
formulas: pH < 7 = acidic, pH = 7 = neutral, pH > 7 = basic

faq.question: What is pH?
faq.answer: pH is a logarithmic scale measuring hydrogen ion concentration in a solution. It ranges from 0 (most acidic) to 14 (most basic), with 7 being neutral (pure water). Each unit represents a 10× change in acidity.

faq.question: Why is the pH scale logarithmic?
faq.answer: Hydrogen ion concentrations span many orders of magnitude (10⁰ to 10⁻¹⁴ mol/L). A logarithmic scale compresses this range into a manageable 0-14 scale. A pH change of 1 means a 10× change in H⁺ concentration.

faq.question: What pH is safe for drinking water?
faq.answer: The EPA recommends drinking water pH between 6.5 and 8.5. Most tap water is 7.0-8.0. Highly acidic water (below 6.5) can corrode pipes and leach metals. Highly alkaline water may taste bitter.

keywords: dilution calculator, c1v1 c2v2, solution dilution, concentration calculator
calcFn: var calcV1 = (c2 * v2) / c1; var solv = v2 - calcV1; var df = c1 / c2; document.getElementById('solveV1').textContent = fmt(calcV1, 2) + ' mL'; document.getElementById('solvent').textContent = fmt(solv, 2) + ' mL'; document.getElementById('dilFactor').textContent = fmt(df, 1) + '×';

faq.question: What is the dilution equation?
faq.answer: C1V1 = C2V2, where C1 is stock concentration, V1 is stock volume needed, C2 is desired concentration, and V2 is desired final volume. This assumes the solute amount stays constant during dilution.

faq.question: How do serial dilutions work?
faq.answer: Serial dilutions are repeated dilutions of the same factor. For a 1:10 serial dilution, take 1 mL of solution and add 9 mL of solvent, then repeat with the new solution. Each step reduces concentration by 10×.

faq.question: Does C1V1=C2V2 work for all solutions?
faq.answer: It works well for dilute solutions. For concentrated acids or solutions that change volume on mixing (non-ideal solutions), it becomes approximate. Always add acid to water (never water to acid) for safety.

tagline: Calculate radioactive decay and remaining material
keywords: half life calculator, radioactive decay, nuclear decay calculator, isotope half life
calcFn: var hl = elapsed / halfLife; var rem = initial * Math.pow(0.5, hl); var dec = initial - rem; document.getElementById('remaining').textContent = fmt(rem, 4); document.getElementById('decayed').textContent = fmt(dec, 4); document.getElementById('halfLives').textContent = fmt(hl, 2); document.getElementById('pctRemaining').textContent = pct(rem / initial * 100, 2);

faq.question: What is a half-life?
faq.answer: A half-life is the time required for half of a radioactive substance to decay. Carbon-14 has a half-life of 5,730 years. Uranium-238: 4.5 billion years. Iodine-131: 8 days. The concept also applies to drug metabolism.

faq.question: How is half-life used in carbon dating?
faq.answer: Living organisms absorb Carbon-14 from the atmosphere. After death, C-14 decays with a 5,730-year half-life. By measuring the remaining C-14 ratio, scientists can date organic materials up to ~50,000 years old.

faq.question: Does half-life mean it's all gone after 2 half-lives?
faq.answer: No. Each half-life reduces the amount by half. After 1: 50% remains. After 2: 25%. After 3: 12.5%. After 10: 0.1%. The material never fully disappears mathematically, but after ~10 half-lives it's effectively negligible.

keywords: ideal gas law calculator, pv=nrt calculator, gas law calculator, chemistry gas calculator
calcFn: var R = 0.08206; var T = tempC + 273.15; if (moles === 0 && pressure > 0 && volume > 0) { var n = (pressure * volume) / (R * T); document.getElementById('solvedVar').textContent = 'n = ' + fmt(n, 4) + ' mol'; } else if (pressure === 0 && moles > 0 && volume > 0) { var P = (moles * R * T) / volume; document.getElementById('solvedVar').textContent = 'P = ' + fmt(P, 4) + ' atm'; } else if (volume === 0 && moles > 0 && pressure > 0) { var V = (moles * R * T) / pressure; document.getElementById('solvedVar').textContent = 'V = ' + fmt(V, 4) + ' L'; } else { var n2 = (pressure * volume) / (R * T); document.getElementById('solvedVar').textContent = 'n = ' + fmt(n2, 4) + ' mol'; } document.getElementById('tempK').textContent = fmt(T, 2) + ' K'; var densAir = (pressure * 28.97) / (R * T); document.getElementById('densityEst').textContent = fmt(densAir, 4) + ' g/L';
formulas: Set the unknown variable to 0 to solve for it

faq.question: What is the ideal gas law?
faq.answer: PV = nRT relates pressure (P), volume (V), moles of gas (n), and temperature (T) through the gas constant R. It assumes gas molecules don't interact and have negligible volume — a good approximation for most gases at normal conditions.

faq.question: What is STP in chemistry?
faq.answer: Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP) is 0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm. At STP, one mole of an ideal gas occupies 22.4 liters. This is a standard reference condition for comparing gas properties.

faq.question: When does the ideal gas law fail?
faq.answer: It becomes inaccurate at high pressures (gases compress), low temperatures (near liquefaction), and for polar molecules (strong intermolecular forces). For these conditions, use the Van der Waals equation or other real gas models.

tagline: Calculate solution concentration in moles per liter
keywords: molarity calculator, moles per liter, concentration calculator, solution molarity
calcFn: var moles = soluteMass / molarMass; var M = moles / volumeL; var mgml = (soluteMass * 1000) / (volumeL * 1000); document.getElementById('molarity').textContent = fmt(M, 4) + ' M'; document.getElementById('molesCalc').textContent = fmt(moles, 4) + ' mol'; document.getElementById('mgPerMl').textContent = fmt(mgml, 2) + ' mg/mL';

faq.question: What is molarity?
faq.answer: Molarity (M) is the number of moles of solute per liter of solution. A 1 M NaCl solution contains 58.44 grams of NaCl per liter. It's the most common way to express concentration in chemistry.

faq.question: How is molarity different from molality?
faq.answer: Molarity (M) = moles per liter of solution. Molality (m) = moles per kilogram of solvent. Molality doesn't change with temperature (since mass doesn't change), while molarity does (since volume changes with temperature).

faq.question: What is a common molar mass to know?
faq.answer: NaCl: 58.44 g/mol. Water: 18.02 g/mol. Glucose: 180.16 g/mol. NaOH: 40.00 g/mol. HCl: 36.46 g/mol. Molar mass equals the atomic masses on the periodic table added together.

tagline: Convert between wavelength, frequency, and energy
keywords: wavelength calculator, frequency calculator, wavelength to frequency, electromagnetic spectrum
calcFn: var c = 2.998e8; var h = 6.626e-34; var wlM, freq; if (direction === 'wl') { wlM = wavelength * 1e-9; freq = c / wlM; } else { freq = wavelength * 1e12; wlM = c / freq; } var E = h * (c / wlM); var wlNm = wlM * 1e9; var region = wlNm < 10 ? 'X-Ray / Gamma' : wlNm < 380 ? 'Ultraviolet' : wlNm < 450 ? 'Violet' : wlNm < 495 ? 'Blue' : wlNm < 570 ? 'Green' : wlNm < 590 ? 'Yellow' : wlNm < 620 ? 'Orange' : wlNm < 750 ? 'Red' : wlNm < 1e6 ? 'Infrared' : 'Microwave / Radio'; document.getElementById('freqResult').textContent = (c/wlM).toExponential(3) + ' Hz'; document.getElementById('wlResult').textContent = fmt(wlNm, 2) + ' nm'; document.getElementById('energy').textContent = E.toExponential(3) + ' J (' + fmt(E * 6.242e18, 2) + ' eV)'; document.getElementById('spectrum').textContent = region;
formulas: c = λ × f (speed of light = wavelength × frequency)

faq.question: What is the relationship between wavelength and frequency?
faq.answer: They are inversely proportional: as wavelength increases, frequency decreases. Their product always equals the speed of light (c = λf). Visible light ranges from 380 nm (violet, high frequency) to 700 nm (red, low frequency).

faq.question: What are the visible light wavelengths?
faq.answer: Violet: 380-450 nm. Blue: 450-495 nm. Green: 495-570 nm. Yellow: 570-590 nm. Orange: 590-620 nm. Red: 620-700 nm. Below 380 nm is ultraviolet, above 700 nm is infrared.

faq.question: What does photon energy mean?
faq.answer: Each photon carries energy proportional to its frequency (E = hf). Higher frequency light (blue, UV) has more energetic photons. This is why UV light causes sunburn but visible light doesn't — UV photons have enough energy to damage DNA.

keywords: acceleration calculator, f=ma calculator, physics calculator, kinematics calculator
calcFn: var a = force / mass; var vf = vi + a * time; var d = vi * time + 0.5 * a * time * time; var g = a / 9.81; document.getElementById('accel').textContent = fmt(a, 2) + ' m/s²'; document.getElementById('finalV').textContent = fmt(vf, 2) + ' m/s (' + fmt(vf * 3.6, 1) + ' km/h)'; document.getElementById('distance').textContent = fmt(d, 2) + ' m'; document.getElementById('gForce').textContent = fmt(g, 2) + ' g';

faq.question: What is acceleration?
faq.answer: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity over time, measured in m/s². An acceleration of 10 m/s² means speed increases by 10 m/s every second. Deceleration is negative acceleration.

faq.question: What is Newton's Second Law?
faq.answer: F = ma states that force equals mass times acceleration. A 10 kg object experiencing 100 N of force accelerates at 10 m/s². Doubling the mass halves the acceleration for the same force.

faq.question: What is a g-force?
faq.answer: One g is Earth's gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²). A roller coaster at 3g means you feel 3× your body weight. Fighter pilots experience up to 9g. Humans typically lose consciousness around 5-6g sustained.

tagline: Calculate satellite orbital speed and period
keywords: orbital velocity calculator, satellite speed, orbital period, space orbit calculator
calcFn: var G = 6.674e-11; var bodies = {earth: {M: 5.972e24, R: 6371}, moon: {M: 7.348e22, R: 1737}, mars: {M: 6.417e23, R: 3390}}; var b = bodies[bodyMass] || bodies.earth; var r = (b.R + altitude) * 1000; var v = Math.sqrt(G * b.M / r); var T = 2 * Math.PI * r / v; var orbits = 86400 / T; document.getElementById('velocity').textContent = fmt(v, 0) + ' m/s (' + fmt(v * 3.6, 0) + ' km/h)'; document.getElementById('period').textContent = fmt(T / 60, 1) + ' minutes'; document.getElementById('orbitsPerDay').textContent = fmt(orbits, 2);

faq.question: What is orbital velocity?
faq.answer: Orbital velocity is the speed needed to maintain a stable orbit at a given altitude. At 400 km above Earth (ISS altitude), it's about 7,660 m/s (27,600 km/h). Higher orbits require lower velocity but longer periods.

faq.question: How fast does the ISS travel?
faq.answer: The ISS orbits at about 7,660 m/s (17,130 mph) at 408 km altitude, completing one orbit every 92 minutes — about 15.5 orbits per day. Astronauts see 15-16 sunrises and sunsets daily.

faq.question: What is geostationary orbit?
faq.answer: Geostationary orbit (GEO) is at 35,786 km altitude where orbital period equals Earth's rotation (24 hours). Satellites here appear stationary above one spot. Speed is about 3,075 m/s. Used for TV, weather, and communications satellites.
